content_en:
Among the Old Testament passages that Muslim scholars have identified as prophesying the coming of Muhammad (peace be upon him), Deuteronomy 18:18 holds a position of particular prominence. In this verse, God speaks to Moses and says: 'I will raise up for them a prophet like you from among their brothers. I will put my words in his mouth, and he will tell them everything I command him.' Ahmad Deedat and other Muslim scholars have argued that this prophecy, examined carefully in its historical, textual, and contextual dimensions, points with considerable specificity to the Prophet Muhammad.

The key phrase 'a prophet like you' — like Moses — is central to this argument. Deedat identifies multiple points of comparison between Moses and Muhammad that set both of them apart from Jesus: Moses was born of two human parents, Muhammad was born of two human parents, while Jesus is claimed by Christianity to have been born without a human father. Moses married and had children, Muhammad married and had children, while Jesus in the Christian tradition remained celibate. Moses led a political community and established civil law, Muhammad led a political community and established civil and religious law, while Jesus did not establish a state or civil legal code. Moses died a natural death and was buried, Muhammad died a natural death and was buried, while the Christian claim for Jesus involves resurrection after crucifixion. These parallels, Deedat argues, make Muhammad far more eligible than Jesus to be identified as the prophet 'like Moses' described in Deuteronomy.

The phrase 'from among their brothers' is also significant. Moses was from the Children of Israel, and the prophecy speaks of a prophet coming from 'their brothers' — a formulation that would naturally refer to a people related to but distinct from the Israelites. The Arabs, and specifically the descendants of Ismail (Ishmael), fit this description precisely: Ishmael was the brother of Isaac, from whom the Israelites descended, making the Ishmaelites — among whom Muhammad was born — the literal 'brothers' of the Israelites referenced in this prophecy.

The description 'I will put my words in his mouth' is particularly apt for the manner in which Quranic revelation was received. The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) was an unlettered man — he could not read or write — and the Quran was revealed to him through the Angel Jibril. His role was to receive and transmit the exact divine words, not to compose or interpret them. He is often described in the hadith literature as repeating after the Angel, memorizing the exact words of revelation, and delivering them to his Companions without alteration. The image of God putting his words in the prophet's mouth is therefore a remarkably precise description of the mechanics of Quranic revelation.

Christian biblical interpreters have traditionally argued that Deuteronomy 18:18 refers to Jesus or to the entire line of Israelite prophets rather than to any single future figure. Deedat engages with these interpretations carefully, noting that the verse's specificity — 'a prophet like you,' singular — and the characteristics it describes do not fit the interpretation that refers it to the prophetic tradition as a whole. He also notes that the Gospel of John records the Jews of Jerusalem asking John the Baptist: 'Are you the Prophet?' (1:21) — a question that implies they expected a single specific prophet to come, distinct from both the Messiah and Elijah. This 'Prophet' expected by the Jews of the first century, Deedat argues, was precisely the figure described in Deuteronomy 18:18.

The broader theological point is that the prophetic tradition did not end with Moses or even with Jesus but was fulfilled and completed in the final messenger to humanity, Muhammad (peace be upon him). The Old Testament prophetic literature, read attentively and without the presumption that its fulfillment must lie exclusively within the Israelite or Christian communities, reveals a trajectory that culminates in the Prophet of Islam.
content_id:
Di antara bagian-bagian Perjanjian Lama yang oleh para ulama Muslim telah diidentifikasi sebagai bernubuat tentang kedatangan Muhammad ﷺ, Ulangan 18:18 memegang posisi yang sangat menonjol. Dalam ayat ini, Allah berbicara kepada Musa dan berkata: 'Seorang nabi akan Kubangkitkan bagi mereka dari antara saudara mereka, seperti engkau ini; Aku akan menaruh firman-Ku dalam mulutnya, dan ia akan mengatakan kepada mereka segala yang Kuperintahkan kepadanya.' Ahmad Deedat dan ulama-ulama Muslim lainnya telah berargumen bahwa nubuatan ini, jika dikaji dengan cermat dalam dimensi historis, tekstual, dan kontekstualnya, menunjuk dengan spesifisitas yang cukup besar kepada Nabi Muhammad ﷺ.

Frasa kunci 'seorang nabi seperti engkau' — seperti Musa — adalah inti dari argumen ini. Deedat mengidentifikasi berbagai titik perbandingan antara Musa dan Muhammad yang membedakan keduanya dari Isa: Musa lahir dari dua orang tua manusia, Muhammad lahir dari dua orang tua manusia, sementara Isa diklaim oleh Kristen telah lahir tanpa ayah manusia. Musa menikah dan mempunyai anak, Muhammad menikah dan mempunyai anak, sementara Isa dalam tradisi Kristen tetap selibat. Musa memimpin komunitas politik dan menetapkan hukum sipil, Muhammad memimpin komunitas politik dan menetapkan hukum sipil dan agama, sementara Isa tidak mendirikan negara atau kode hukum sipil. Musa meninggal dengan kematian alami dan dikuburkan, Muhammad meninggal dengan kematian alami dan dikuburkan, sementara klaim Kristen tentang Isa melibatkan kebangkitan setelah penyaliban. Kesejajaran-kesejajaran ini, Deedat berargumen, membuat Muhammad jauh lebih memenuhi syarat daripada Isa untuk diidentifikasi sebagai nabi 'seperti Musa' yang digambarkan dalam Ulangan.

Frasa 'dari antara saudara mereka' juga signifikan. Musa berasal dari Bani Israel, dan nubuatan itu berbicara tentang seorang nabi yang datang dari 'saudara-saudara mereka' — formulasi yang secara alami merujuk kepada orang-orang yang terkait tetapi berbeda dari Bani Israel. Orang-orang Arab, dan khususnya keturunan Ismail, sesuai dengan deskripsi ini dengan tepat: Ismail adalah saudara laki-laki Ishaq, dari siapa Bani Israel berasal, menjadikan orang-orang Ismail — di antaranya Muhammad lahir — sebagai 'saudara-saudara' harfiah dari Bani Israel yang dirujuk dalam nubuatan ini.

Deskripsi 'Aku akan menaruh firman-Ku dalam mulutnya' sangat tepat untuk cara diterimanya wahyu Al-Quran. Nabi Muhammad ﷺ adalah seorang yang ummi — ia tidak dapat membaca atau menulis — dan Al-Quran diwahyukan kepadanya melalui Malaikat Jibril. Perannya adalah menerima dan menyampaikan firman-firman ilahi yang tepat, bukan untuk mengarang atau menafsirkannya. Ia sering digambarkan dalam literatur hadis sebagai mengulang setelah Malaikat, menghafal kata-kata persis wahyu, dan menyampaikannya kepada para Sahabat tanpa perubahan. Gambaran Allah menaruh firman-Nya dalam mulut sang nabi oleh karena itu adalah deskripsi yang sangat tepat tentang mekanisme wahyu Al-Quran.

Para penafsir alkitabiah Kristen secara tradisional telah berargumen bahwa Ulangan 18:18 merujuk kepada Isa atau kepada seluruh rangkaian nabi-nabi Israel daripada kepada satu tokoh masa depan yang tunggal. Deedat terlibat dengan interpretasi-interpretasi ini dengan cermat, mencatat bahwa spesifisitas ayat — 'seorang nabi seperti engkau,' tunggal — dan karakteristik-karakteristik yang digambarkannya tidak sesuai dengan interpretasi yang merujuknya kepada tradisi kenabian secara keseluruhan. Ia juga mencatat bahwa Injil Yohanes mencatat orang-orang Yahudi Yerusalem yang bertanya kepada Yohanes Pembaptis: 'Apakah engkau nabi itu?' (1:21) — sebuah pertanyaan yang menyiratkan bahwa mereka mengharapkan seorang nabi khusus yang akan datang, berbeda dari Mesias maupun Elia. 'Nabi' ini yang diharapkan oleh orang-orang Yahudi abad pertama, Deedat berargumen, adalah tepat tokoh yang digambarkan dalam Ulangan 18:18.

Poin teologi yang lebih luas adalah bahwa tradisi kenabian tidak berakhir dengan Musa atau bahkan dengan Isa tetapi dipenuhi dan diselesaikan dalam utusan terakhir kepada umat manusia, Muhammad ﷺ. Literatur kenabian Perjanjian Lama, jika dibaca dengan penuh perhatian dan tanpa anggapan bahwa pemenuhannya harus terletak secara eksklusif dalam komunitas-komunitas Israel atau Kristen, mengungkapkan sebuah trajektori yang memuncak pada Nabi Islam.
content_ar: Among the Old Testament passages that مسلم العلماء have identified as prophesying the coming of محمد ﷺ (ﷺ), Deuteronomy 18:18 holds a position of particular prominence. In this الآية, God speaks to موسى and says: 'I will raise up for them a النبي like you from among their brothers. I will put my words in his mouth, and he will tell them everything I command him.' أحمد Deedat and other مسلم العلماء have argued that this prophecy, examined carefully in its historical, textual, and contextual dimensions, points with considerable specificity to النبي ﷺ محمد ﷺ. The الرئيسي phrase 'a النبي like you' — like موسى — is المحوري to this argument. Deedat identifies multiple points of comparison between موسى and محمد ﷺ that set both of them apart from عيسى ﷺ: موسى was born of two human الوالدان, محمد ﷺ was born of two human الوالدان, بينما عيسى ﷺ is claimed by Christianity to have been born without a human الأب. موسى married and had الأولاد, محمد ﷺ married and had الأولاد, بينما عيسى ﷺ in the Christian التراث remained celibate. موسى led a political الجماعة and established civil law, محمد ﷺ led a political الجماعة and established civil and religious law, بينما عيسى ﷺ did not establish a state or civil legal code. موسى died a natural الموت and was buried, محمد ﷺ died a natural الموت and was buried, بينما the Christian claim for عيسى ﷺ involves البعث بعد crucifixion. These parallels, Deedat argues, make محمد ﷺ far more eligible than عيسى ﷺ to be identified as النبي ﷺ 'like موسى' described in Deuteronomy. The phrase 'from among their brothers' is أيضاً المهم. موسى was from the الأولاد of Israel, and the prophecy speaks of a النبي coming from 'their brothers' — a formulation that would naturally refer to a people related to but distinct from the Israelites. The Arabs, and specifically the descendants of Ismail (Ishmael), fit this description precisely: Ishmael was the الأخ of Isaac, from whom the Israelites descended, making the Ishmaelites — among whom محمد ﷺ was born — the literal 'brothers' of the Israelites referenced in this prophecy. The description 'I will put my words in his mouth' is particularly apt for the manner in which القرآني الوحي was received. النبي ﷺ محمد ﷺ (ﷺ) was an unlettered man — he could not read or write — and the القرآن الكريم was revealed to him through the Angel جبريل ﷺ. His role was to receive and transmit the exact divine words, not to compose or interpret them. He is often described in the الحديث literature as repeating بعد the Angel, memorizing the exact words of الوحي, and delivering them to his الصحابة without alteration. The image of God putting his words in النبي ﷺ's mouth is لذلك a remarkably precise description of the mechanics of القرآني الوحي. Christian biblical interpreters have traditionally argued that Deuteronomy 18:18 refers to عيسى ﷺ or to the entire line of Israelite
